source.title: Chemical Reaction, Hydrogen Release, Explosion, and Fire at AB Specialty Silicones

event.incident_type: Chemical reaction, hydrogen release, explosion, and fire
actions_taken: Shift Supervisor 1 directed workers to ventilate the hazy vapor
Operator 2 was asked to open a garage door near the locker room
Operator 3 was directed to turn on exhaust fans in the High Bay
Waukegan Fire Department and mutual aid responded
Illinois EPA, EPA, OSHA, and CSB investigated the incident
event.summary: On May 3, 2019, at AB Specialty Silicones in Waukegan, Illinois, an operator pumped an incorrect chemical (10% KOH) into a batch tank containing XL 10 and TD 6/12 Blend. The incompatible chemicals were stored in nearly identical 55-gallon blue drums, differing only in small labels. The reaction between XL 10 (containing silicon hydride bonds) and aqueous KOH produced hydrogen gas, which released into the production building. The hydrogen gas ignited, causing a massive explosion and fire that fatally injured four employees (Byron Biehn, Jeffrey Cummings, Daniel Nicklas, Allen Stevens), destroyed the production building, and forced operational disruptions.
recommendations: Amend OSHA PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, including broadening application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals
Revise EPA Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals
Ensure hydrogen gas detection and alarm systems are properly installed, maintained, and configured based on facility application, manufacturer specifications, codes, standards, and industry guidance
Establish a safety management system addressing process safety at AB Specialty Waukegan facility, including elements from CCPS publications
Incorporate CCPS Essential Practices for Managing Chemical Reactivity Hazards into operations
key_phrases: storage and handling of incompatible materials
batch equipment and ventilation system design
regulatory coverage gap for reactive hazards

hazards.name: Silicon hydride reactivity with aqueous bases
Hydrogen gas generation from chemical reaction
Flammable gas accumulation in enclosed space

threats.name: Operator error in chemical identification and addition
Similar appearance of incompatible chemical drums
Inadequate hazard analysis of batch process
Failure of double initial procedure program
Hydrogen gas release into production building
Ignition of hydrogen gas in explosive concentration

controls.name: Double Initial Procedure Program
controls.barrier_role: Prevent wrong material addition to batch
controls.lod_basis: Procedural control requiring two employees to verify chemical identity and initial batch ticket before charging

supporting_text: AB Specialty developed a double initial procedure practice in 2014 in an effort to prevent employees from charging the wrong materials to batch processes
The occurrence of the May 3 incident indicates that AB Specialty's double initial procedure program did not prevent a wrong material from being added to the tank
Operator 1 likely misidentified the 10% KOH drum(s) that remained in the emulsions area after the first batch for XL 10 and added 10% KOH into the tank

controls.name: Chemical Drum Labeling and Identification
controls.barrier_role: Enable operator identification of correct chemical
controls.lod_basis: Reliance on small text labels affixed to drum sides to differentiate incompatible chemicals

supporting_text: AB Specialty relied on its operators to confirm drum content based on the label affixed to the side of the drum, which used small text for the chemical name

The incompatible chemicals that were mixed were stored in similar 55-gallon blue plastic drums. The similar appearance of the drums likely contributed to the operator adding the incorrect chemical to the tank
Once these bung caps were removed, the drums looked nearly identical

controls.name: Segregation of Incompatible Chemical Drums
controls.barrier_role: Prevent co-location of incompatible chemicals in work area
controls.lod_basis: Written procedure requiring segregation of incompatible chemicals and removal of containers after use

supporting_text: AB Specialty did not have a written procedure requiring employees to segregate the 10% KOH and XL 10 drums in the emulsions area or to remove ingredient containers from the emulsions area after use

The CSB determined there were likely also two or three drums containing 10% KOH in the production area, possibly leftover from the previous EM 652 batch operation. As a result, there were up to eleven nearly identical drums containing incompatible chemicals (XL 10 and 10% KOH) in the immediate process area at the start of the second batch

controls.name: Hazard Analysis Program (Technical Service Requests)
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and establish safeguards for batch processes
controls.lod_basis: TSR process intended to assess safety-related questions including product hazards and engineering controls

supporting_text: AB Specialty assessed proposed product manufacturing operations through what it called technical service requests (TSRs)

Spreadsheets provided to the CSB from AB Specialty indicate the EM 652 product underwent the TSR process in October 2014 and June 2018. No hazards or safeguards were documented on either TSR spreadsheet
AB Specialty's TSR process did not and was not intended to assess the hazards of performing a process operation or establish safeguards to reduce risk

controls.name: Batch Tank Vent Pipe Design
controls.barrier_role: Direct gases produced during reaction to safe location outside building
controls.lod_basis: Engineering design feature to prevent gas release into occupied work area

supporting_text: As a result of the tanks used in the EM 652 batch process having an open hatch-type lid and no vent pipe to direct gases to a safe location, the hydrogen gas produced during the incident released directly into the production building, where workers were located

While some of the tanks used for the emulsion processes were equipped with vent pipes to divert any produced gases outside the building or to a process ventilation system, most of the tanks were not designed with this feature, and the tank in which the reaction occurred on the night of the incident was not equipped with a vent pipe

controls.name: Hydrogen Gas Detection and Alarm System
controls.barrier_role: Detect hydrogen gas release and alert workers to evacuate
controls.lod_basis: Fixed gas detection system with alarm to warn of hazardous atmosphere

supporting_text: The AB Specialty production building did not have a hydrogen gas or flammable gas detection and alarm system to warn employees of a hazardous atmosphere

The lack of a system to detect hydrogen gas and automatically activate an alarm contributed to personnel remaining inside the production building between the start of the hydrogen release and the time of ignition
AB Specialty employees told the CSB that the facility had installed two fixed lower explosive limit (LEL) gas detector systems in the Low Bay around October 2018 for a trial evaluation
Around March 2019, during a scheduled maintenance activity, AB Specialty employees found that the two gas detectors had failed; they had unresponsive sensors

controls.name: Batch Ticket Warning of XL 10 Reactivity
controls.barrier_role: Inform operators of reactivity hazard with acids and bases
controls.lod_basis: Written warning on batch ticket documenting XL 10 reactivity with acids and bases and hydrogen generation

supporting_text: The batch ticket for the EM 652 batch process warned that XL 10 was reactive with acids and bases and could generate hydrogen
Operators who worked with the EM 652 batch process were also made aware of the XL 10 reactivity with acids and bases by a warning note contained in the EM 652 batch ticket

controls.name: Emergency Preparedness Training and Drills
controls.barrier_role: Prepare workers to recognize and respond to process emergencies
controls.lod_basis: Annual emergency training and drills to prepare personnel for process upsets

supporting_text: AB Specialty also provided employees with training on what to do during process emergencies, performing yearly emergency drills and critiques with their personnel

During the incident, workers recognized that a process upset had occurred when the tank contents foamed, overflowed the tank, and a fog formed. However, despite recognizing the process upset, the workers did not recognize the immediate hydrogen hazard created by the upset

Hydrogen is a colorless and odorless gas indistinguishable from air without the use of additional technology, such as gas detectors. Without gas detectors and alarms alerting of the hazardous conditions, or effective training, the workers did not realize the necessity to evacuate

controls.name: Ventilation System Operation
controls.barrier_role: Remove hydrogen gas from production building
controls.lod_basis: Mechanical ventilation system with exhaust fans to remove hazardous gases

supporting_text: The ventilation system, including an air mover—designed to introduce outside air to the building and which was positioned near the location where the batch operation was being performed—may have helped distribute the hydrogen in the production building and mix it with air, creating a large and explosive gas cloud

Shift Supervisor 1 directed workers to take action to ventilate the hazy vapor from the building. He asked Operator 2 to open a garage door near the locker room. Shift Supervisor 1 also told Operator 3 to go to the maintenance area and turn on the switch for the fans
The High Bay end exhaust fans did not run continuously and had to be manually activated by a switch located in the maintenance area

controls.name: Process Safety Management System
controls.barrier_role: Establish comprehensive safety management framework for reactive chemical processes
controls.lod_basis: Formal safety management system addressing process safety elements per industry guidance

supporting_text: AB Specialty did not have a safety management system that addressed process safety in place at the time of the incident
Industry best practice publications provide guidance on establishing process safety management systems for facilities with known or potential reactive chemical hazards

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
